coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of heteroatom Doped-Carbon dot graphene oxide nanocomposites and investigation of nonlinear optical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Bingxin
Liang, Chen
Li, Jian
Dong, Wenyue
Cui, Xu
Duan, Qian
coredata.dc:description: A series of carbon dot graphene oxide (CDGO) nanocomposites and heteroatom doped-carbon dot graphene oxide (HD-CDGO) nanocomposites were prepared by one-step electrochemical exfoliation methods, where heteroatom represents P, N and S atoms. TEM, FT-IR and XPS were performed to confirm the structure of HD-CDGO. The Z-scan tests were used to investigate the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of HD-CDGO, and the estimation of the band gap was performed using UV–vis and Tauc plots. The results demonstrate that the energy gaps of the prepared HD-CDGO were reduced, and the NLO properties were improved compared with those of CDGO. The nonlinear absorption coefficient (β eff) of S-CDGO is 9.3 times higher than that of CDGO, and the imaginary part of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility (Im χ(3)) increases from 23 × 10-12 esu to 213 × 10-12 esu. Furthermore, different sulfur element concentration doped CDGO (Sx-CDGO, x = 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, S0.10-CDGO equals S-CDGO) were prepared, and the energy gap of Sx-CDGO decreases from 4.5 eV to 2.5 eV as the surface sulfur atomic concentration of Sx-CDGO increases from 0.6 % to 1.8 %, the β eff value of Sx-CDGO increases from 15 × 10-10 to 65 × 10-10 m/W. Moreover, HD-CDGO/polymethyl methacrylate (HD-CDGO/PMMA) composites were prepared for practical applications. S-CDGO/PMMA composites exhibit the best NLO property, with the initial threshold value and limiting threshold of 0.05 and 0.40 J cm−2, respectively, and the minimum transmittance of 21 %. Our work provides a promising design strategy to synthesize high-performance optical limiting (OL) materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heteroatom doped-carbon dot graphene oxide
Nonlinear optical materials
Optical limiting
Electrochemical exfoliation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural properties of synthesized of CDGO and HD-CDGO
Optical properties of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO
OL properties of S-CDGO/PMMA, P-CDGO/PMMA and N-CDGO/PMMA composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Jilin Science & Technology Department (20190303069SF) for the financial support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of S-CDGO (a), P-CDGO (b) and N-CDGO (c) and corresponding elemental mapping images of S-CDGO (d), P-CDGO (e) and N-CDGO (f), inset is the respective HRTEM images.
XPS fine spectra of S 2p for S-CDGO (a), P 2p for P-CDGO (b) and N 1 s for N-CDGO (c).
FT-IR spectra of CDGO (a), S-CDGO (b), P-CDGO (c) and N-CDGO (d).
X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) patterns of CDGO (a), S-CDGO (b), P-CDGO (c) and N-CDGO (d).
Raman spectra of CDGO (a), S-CDGO (b), P-CDGO (c) and N-CDGO (d).
The open aperture Z-scan tests of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO (in DMF, 0.15 g/L).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) Tauc plots for the determination of the energy gap of CDGO , S-CDGO , P-CDGO and N-CDGO (in DMF, 0.15 g/L). (c) Energy gaps (red) and β eff values (blue) of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO.
TEM images of S0.05-CDGO (a), S0.10-CDGO (b), S0.20-CDGO (c), S0.40-CDGO (d); and HRTEM images of S0.05-CDGO (e), S0.10-CDGO (f), S0.20-CDGO (g) and S0.40-CDGO (h).

(a) The open aperture Z-scan tests of the Sx-CDGO (in DMF, 0.15 g/L). (b) Tauc plot for determination of the energy gap of Sx-CDGO (in DMF, 0.15 g/L). (c) Energy gap (red) and β eff values (blue) of Sx-CDGO. (d) Raman spectra of Sx-CDGO.
OL properties (a) and nonlinear transmittance (b) of S-CDGO/PMMA , P-CDGO/PMMA and N-CDGO/PMMA composites.
Schematic diagram of the experimental setup.
NLO properties with CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO in DMF.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of heteroatom Doped-Carbon dot graphene oxide nanocomposites and investigation of nonlinear optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Optoelectronic Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Changchun, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Optoelectronic Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Optoelectronic Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Changchun, 130022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of carbon dot graphene oxide (CDGO) nanocomposites and heteroatom doped-carbon dot graphene oxide (HD-CDGO) nanocomposites were prepared by one-step electrochemical exfoliation methods, where heteroatom represents P, N and S atoms. TEM, FT-IR and XPS were performed to confirm the structure of HD-CDGO. The Z-scan tests were used to investigate the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of HD-CDGO, and the estimation of the band gap was performed using UV–vis and Tauc plots. The results demonstrate that the energy gaps of the prepared HD-CDGO were reduced, and the NLO properties were improved compared with those of CDGO. The nonlinear absorption coefficient (β eff) of S-CDGO is 9.3 times higher than that of CDGO, and the imaginary part of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility (Im χ(3)) increases from 23 × 10-12 esu to 213 × 10-12 esu. Furthermore, different sulfur element concentration doped CDGO (Sx-CDGO, x = 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, S0.10-CDGO equals S-CDGO) were prepared, and the energy gap of Sx-CDGO decreases from 4.5 eV to 2.5 eV as the surface sulfur atomic concentration of Sx-CDGO increases from 0.6 % to 1.8 %, the β eff value of Sx-CDGO increases from 15 × 10-10 to 65 × 10-10 m/W. Moreover, HD-CDGO/polymethyl methacrylate (HD-CDGO/PMMA) composites were prepared for practical applications. S-CDGO/PMMA composites exhibit the best NLO property, with the initial threshold value and limiting threshold of 0.05 and 0.40 J cm−2, respectively, and the minimum transmittance of 21 %. Our work provides a promising design strategy to synthesize high-performance optical limiting (OL) materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, the application of optoelectronic devices is becoming more and more accessible. Nonlinear optical (NLO) materials [1–4] with high laser damage threshold and excellent chemical stability, have attracted considerable research attention due to their potential applications in optical limiting (OL) [5], photonics technology [6], procedural data [7], optical switching and telecommunication [8]. Among numerous NLO materials, carbon dot (CD) [9], including nanodot, polymer dot and quantum dot (QD), have attracted increasing research interest for their remarkable features, such as high mechanical strength, good stability, eco-friendliness and easy availability [10]. Meanwhile, compared with the single component CDs, hybrid CDs materials such as heteroatom doped CDs and multicomponent CDs composites exhibit enhanced NLO properties due to unique structures and the synergistic coupling effects between their components. For example, Zheng et al. [11–12] presented a carbon dot graphene oxide (CDGO) composite synthesized by electrochemical (EC) method using graphite rods as anodes in ultrapure water, finally the composite was introduced into the silica gel glass to obtain monolithic nanocomposites. The CDGO gel glasses offer significantly better NLO properties than CD or GO suspensions, which the imaginary part of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility was 13.9 × 10-12 esu. Dong et al. [13] prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and l-cysteine, which citric acid acts as a carbon source. Compared with other reported CDs, the as-prepared N, S-CDs exhibit a very high fluorescence quantum yield (73 %). Wang et al. [14] prepared nitrogen doped-carbon dots (N-CD) from diaminophenazine by hydrothermal method afterwards. N-CDs showed a switchable NLO behavior from saturable absorption to reverse saturable absorption (RSA). Fabricating CDs and GO into composites and chemical doping may be an effective way to improve the NLO properties of CDGO [15–17]. Nevertheless, there are few reports on the synthesis and research of NLO property of heteroatom-doped carbon material structure, and the heteroatom doped-carbon dot graphene oxide (HD-CDGO) nanocomposites have not been studied so far, it seems that HD-CDGO are expected to be suitable NLO materials for OL application.

In order to synthesize hybrid CDs materials, synthetic method including chemical vapor deposition [18], chemical corrosion [19], organic carbonization [20–21], and solvothermal synthesis [22] have been used in recent years. However, these methods typically require special equipment, tedious processes, considerable energy consumption, high cost, or harsh synthesis conditions, which limit the large-scale application of heteroatom doped-nanocarbons [23]. To change the above situation, EC exfoliation has been explored for the preparation of CDs, which owns the advantages of moderate conditions, good reproducibility, large-scale production and simple equipment [24]. For instance, Li et al. [25] published a rapid and simple EC exfoliation method of preparing large-scale CDs with salt as the electrolyte. Coincidentally, EC exfoliation also offers a way to prepare different heteroatom doped-CDs materials by using the corresponding electrolytes during the EC process. In this case, Peng et al. [26] applied a facile EC method to synthesize S, P-CDs using a graphite rod as carbon sources. However, few works on the preparation of CDGO by EC method have been reported. Hence, we choose EC method to synthesize CDGO.

In this work, we reported the simultaneous preparation of CDGO composites and doping of heteroatoms by EC method in a one pot reaction. HD-CDGO nanocomposites, where heteroatom represents P, N and S atoms, were synthesized through simple and rapid EC exfoliation, defined as P-CDGO, N-CDGO and S-CDGO, respectively. Nanocomposites and heteroatom doping are prepared in one-pot reactions. Meanwhile, different sulfur element concentrations doped CDGO (Sx-CDGO, x = 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, S0.10-CDGO equals S-CDGO) were also prepared to further investigate the influence of doping concentration on the NLO properties. The open aperture Z-scan tests were used to probe the NLO properties of HD-CDGO. In addition, HD-CDGO/polymethyl methacrylate (HD-CDGO/PMMA) composites were fabricated for the actual application, where PMMA was chosen as the matrix due to its high transparency and chemical stability. The preparation processes of HD-CDGO nanocomposites and HD-CDGO/PMMA composites are illustrated in Scheme 1 . HD-CDGO nanocomposites and HD-CDGO/PMMA composites were prepared via the same procedure. The HD-CDGO/PMMA composites exhibit outstanding performance and a high laser damage threshold in the OL test.

Typically, A graphite board was inserted into the electrolyte as the anode, parallel to another graphite board as the counter-electrode [12]. The electrolyte was prepared by NaOH (0.10 mol/L) in water (500 mL). Using a direct current power supply, a static potential of 10 V was applied to the two electrodes. During 24 h of continuous stirring, the graphite board of the anode corroded. A brown solution gradually emerged in the reactor. Anode graphite board corrosion resulted in a brown solution. Precipitated graphite fragments were separated by centrifugation at 7000 rpm. The supernatant was put into a dialysis bag (500 Da) and stirred for 72 h before freeze dried to obtain the CDGO as black powder. Yield: 30 mg.

As an example, S-CDGO nanocomposites were synthesized by a similar method as in Section 2.1, replace electrolyte with mixing Na2S (0.10 mol/L) and NaOH (0.10 mol/L) in water (500 mL). The S-CDGO nanocomposites in the supernatant are stabilized in water. P-CDGO and N-CDGO were prepared via the same procedure, with the electrolyte of the mixed solution of Na2HPO4 (0.10 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L) and NH4OH (0.10 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L), respectively. Different sulfur element concentrations doped CDGO (S0.05-CDGO, S0.10-CDGO, S0.20-CDGO and S0.40-CDGO) were obtained in a mixed solution of Na2S (0.05 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L), Na2S (0.10 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L), Na2S (0.20 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L), and Na2S (0.40 mol/L)/NaOH (0.10 mol/L), respectively. S-CDGO was obtained as black powder. Yield: 35 mg.

As an example, the preparation method of S-CDGO/PMMA composites is as follows: As a free radical initiator, a mixture of AIBN (60 mg) and MMA (18.88 g) was prepared. The mixture was stirred at 70 °C until the pre-polymerized solution reached the appropriate viscosity. S-CDGO (0.6 mg) was dispersed into DMF (0.2 mL) and added to the pre-polymerized MMA. After adding the solution to the mold, it was heated for 2 h at 75 °C, 1 h at 85 °C before cooling to room temperature. P-CDGO/PMMA and N-CDGO/PMMA were prepared by the same methodology, replacing S-CDGO with equal amounts of P-CDGO and N-CDGO, respectively. The HD-CDGO/PMMA composites were obtained as 2 mm thick solid grey transparent films.

The measured quantity in open-aperture Z-scan experiments of sample were the normalized transmittance, given by eqn (1) for a Gaussian pulse [27–28].
(1)
T
( z )
=
1
π
q 0
( z )
∫
- ∞
∞
ln

q 0
( z )

τ 2
d
τ
where
(2)
q 0
( z )
=
β eff
I 00
L eff
/
( 1 +
z 2
/
z 0 2
)

The nonlinear absorption coefficient (β eff) could be obtained from the equation. I 00 is the intensity of the beam at the focus. z and z 0 are the sample position with respect to the input intensity and the Rayleigh length, respectively. L eff is the effective thickness of the sample and was given by using eqn (3) [29–30].
(3)
L eff
=
1 -
e
- α L
α
where α is the linear absorption coefficient and L is the thickness of the sample.

The imaginary component of the third order optical susceptibility (Im [χ(3)]) is directly proportional to β eff via eqn (4).
(4)
Im
χ
3
=
n 2
ε 0
c λ
β eff
2 π
where c and n are the speeds of the light in vacuum and the linear refractive index, respectively. ε0 is the permittivity of free space and λ is the wavelength of the laser light.

The optical limiting test is carried out on the Z-scan test platform equipped with a polarization light intensity adjuster (Scheme 2). The sample moves to the focus position of the laser beam (z = 0) through the moving platform. Adjust the polarized light intensity regulator to make the front surface energy of the sample the lowest value that can be detected by the detector. During the test, the laser energy is gradually increased from the lowest value by adjusting the rotation angle of the polarization light intensity adjuster. The incident light energy is detected by D1, and the outgoing light energy is detected by D2, thus obtaining the OL diagram. The initial threshold, limiting threshold and dynamic range obtained by optical limiting test can be used to evaluate the optical limiting performance of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties of synthesized of CDGO and HD-CDGO
ce:section.ce:para: A series of CDGO and HD-CDGO (S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO) were synthesized by EC exfoliation. During this process, H2O is decomposed into hydrogen and hydroxide ions. When the anode is further oxidized, hydroxyl radical and oxygen radical would be produced. These radicals attack graphite surfaces from the edge state/grain boundary and promote the insertion layer. Due to EC oxidation, hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxy functional groups are also attached to the surface of the nanoparticles, and the S2-, HPO4 2-, and NH4 + are condensed with the surface group.

Fig. 1 shows the morphology of the obtained S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO. S-CDGO appears as a sheet structure with black spots (highlighted in yellow circles, Fig. 1a) on the surface, layer structure denotes GO, and black spots with a diameter of less than 10 nm are actually graphene spots (denote CDs) obtained during the EC process [31–32], which indicating successful preparation of CDGO composites. CDs and GO are formed simultaneously during EC process. Even after centrifugation and dialysis, CDs are well distributed on GO surfaces. The EC process results in oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs and GO, which facilitates the formation of CDGO via covalent bonds and ensures the stability of CDGO. [12] Simultaneously, heteroatoms will be doped into CDGO by connecting with active groups or inserting surface defects. The density of CDs (highlighted in yellow circles) on N-CDGO (Fig. 1c,) is much lower than that on P-CDGO (Fig. 1b) and S-CDGO (Fig. 1a). Fig. S1 reveals distributed histograms of the particle size of the different CDs in S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO (Fig. S1). It is well known that CDs smaller than 10 nm are often referred to as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) due to their quantum size effects, edge effects and defect effects [8]. The average diameters of the CDs in S-CDGO and P-CDGO are ∼ 3.7 nm and ∼ 8.2 nm, respectively, and the distribution of the CDs in P-CDGO is not as concentrated as that of S-CDGO. A rare number of CDs are found in N-CDGO, and their particle size distribution is the most uneven, with a universal size of over 10 nm, which are outside the realm of QDs. Corresponding elemental mapping images (Fig. 1d, e, f) confirm that S-CDGO consists of C, O and S elements, P-CDGO consists of C, O, and P elements, and N-CDGO consists of C, O and N elements, demonstrating the successful elemental doping in CDGO.

XPS spectra were used to characterize P-CDGO, N-CDGO and S-CDGO (Fig. 2 ). The S 2p peaks of S-CDGO at 162.7 eV, 164.1 eV, 167.8 eV and 169.2 eV can be assigned to CS(O)2-C and CSC, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 2a. The P 2p peaks at 132.5 eV and 133.9 eV can be attributed to C3PO and C2PO2/CPO3, respectively (Fig. 2b). The N 1 s peaks of N-CDGO in Fig. 2c at 397.6 eV and 399.8 eV can be attributed to pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, respectively, which indicates the formation of covalent bonds between nitrogen and carbon. The XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s for S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO are provided in the Supporting Information (Fig. S2). The XPS results confirm that N, P and S are doped in CDGO by covalent linkages.

The structures of P-CDGO, N-CDGO and S-CDGO were further characterized by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectra of S-CDGO shows peaks at 1160, 890 and 580 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of CSC, CS and CCS, respectively. P-CDGO shows clear stretching peaks of PO and OPO at 1150 and 1115 cm−1, and the stretching peaks of POC are presented at 1006 and 861 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectra of N-CDGO, the distinct peaks at 2340 and 1380 cm−1 could be assigned to the CN bond, and the sharp bands at 1584 cm−1 could be ascribed to the NH vibration. These results indicate S, P, N atoms have been doped into CDGO.

XRD was performed to explore the crystalline structure of the CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO. The sharp and intensive peak centered at 26.7° is attributed to graphite (002) plane, as shown in Fig. 4 , which can be observed in CDGO, demonstrating the higher graphitization degree. The interlayer distance d was calculated using Bragg’s law [33], 2dsinθ = nλ where n is the order of diffraction, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray, d is the interlayer distance, θ is the Bragg angle. The value of d002 corresponds to an interlayer distance of 0.34 nm. The average height of stacking layers in HD-CDGO was calculated from Scherrer's equation, D = 0.9λ/βcosθ, where λ is the wavelength of X-ray, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) and θ is Bragg angle. The average stack heights of S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO are estimated to be 10.35 nm, 18.21 nm and 14.76 nm respectively.

The Raman spectra of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO display a defect (D) band at ∼ 1340 cm−1 and a graphite (G) band at ∼ 1590 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 5 . The G peak is the typical characteristic of the sp2 CC bonds in grapheme, the D band is related to non-sp2 carbon bonding, and tend to indicate lattice disorders and structural defects. Heteroatom doping usually introduces defects in graphene nanosheets. After heteroatom doping, the intensity ratios of D to G peaks (ID/IG) increased from 0.332 for CDGO to 0.735, 0.992 and 0.878 for S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO, respectively. The increased intensity ratios imply the increased defects in the composites. At the same time, according to the previous literature, the degree of graphitization of CDs is extremely low, and the increase in defects may also come from CDs generated on the surface of the composites [34–35]. The ratio of the G peak and 2D peak intensities is often used to infer the number of layers in graphene, and the ratio decreases as the number of layers increases [36–37]. The intensity ratios of I2D/IG are 0.359, 0.149 and 0.275 for S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO, suggesting that S-CDGO has the least number of layers [38–39] which is confirmed by Raman. The results show that S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO were successfully prepared by the EC method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO
ce:section.ce:para: The open aperture Z-scan tests were used to explore the nonlinear absorption (NLA) properties of CDGO, S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO. As shown in Fig. 6 a, a distinctive 'V' type is evident in the transmittance of the composites, a valley within the peak appears at the focus and becomes deeper increasingly, which implies that reverse saturable absorption (RSA) and two-photon absorption (2PA) appears following a NLA transition occurs [40]. In the low energy range, RSA is attributed to excited state absorption (ESA) in all samples, whereas, under nonreasoning excitation 2PA dominates the NLA behavior [41]. 2PA exists in a sp3 matrix and sp2 domains, which are abundant in CDGO and HD-CDGO [27]. The NLA properties of composites in DMF at a concentration of 0.15 g/L are summarized in Table 1 . The properties of HD-CDGO have all been improved compared with CDGO, and S-CDGO exhibits the best β eff value at 65 × 10-10 m W−1.

For the influence of different doping heteroatoms on the NLO properties, the energy gaps of HD-CDGO were studied by UV–vis absorption spectra and Tauc plots estimations (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorption spectra of CDGO are very similar to that of GO. The absorption peak of CDGO at 230 nm is attributed to the highly conjugated structure of graphite [40], and the absorption peaks at approximately 300 nm is attributed to an n-π* transitions of CO bonds. Compared with CDGO, the absorption spectrum of HD-CDGO have a slight red shift, the characteristic shoulder at 300 nm disappears and the corresponding absorbance gradually increases at the same wavelength. It suggested the continuous increase of π-conjugated electron density [42–44], which may be related to the fact that the average stacking height of HD-CDGO is higher than that of CDGO, which is confirmed by XRD results. Fig. 7b shows the spectra plotted as (αhν)2 vs the photoenergy (hν), and the dotted lines are fitting curves to calculate the energy gap. The estimated band gaps of CDGO, N-CDGO, P-CDGO and S-CDGO are approximately 4.6, 3.0, 2.9 and 2.6 eV, respectively. Band structure differences exist in P-CDGO, N-CDGO and S-CDGO, which is ascribable to differences of valence electrons and electronegativities among the P, N, and S atoms. The Fermi level for P-, N- and S-doped graphene is upshifted compared to the monovacancy defective graphene because P, N and S have more available electrons that can replenish some of the electron deficiency [45]. S atom has the most available electrons; therefore, S-doped graphene has the narrowest band gap. Meanwhile, the difference in electronegativity of N atoms and P atoms may be the reason of smaller band gap of P-CDGO than N-CDGO. The relationship between the β eff value and the energy gap for each HD-CDGO is shown in Fig. 7c. The β eff values of CDGO, N-CDGO, P-CDGO and S-CDGO are 7 × 10−10, 13 × 10−10, 18 × 10−10 and 65 × 10−10 m W−1, respectively. S-CDGO has the best NLO properties due to the low energy gap, which is related to the value of the transition energy [46–47]. The lower the energy gap, the easier it is for the electron to reach the excited singlet state from the ground state and the excited triplet state through the intersystem crossing, thus enhancing the RSA behavior. The improvement of RSA behavior leads to the improvement of nonlinear optical properties of materials.

To further investigate the influence of different sulfur doping concentrations on both the energy gap and NLO properties. Based on the best performance of S-CDGO, a series of Sx-CDGO nanocomposites with different sulfur doping concentrations were prepared by adjusting the concentration of sulfur ion in the electrolyte to 0.05, 0.10, 0.20 and 0.40 mol/L, which were defined as S0.05-CDGO, S0.10-CDGO, S0.20-CDGO and S0.40-CDGO, respectively. XPS spectra confirm the presence of S doped in the Sx-CDGO (Fig. S3). Calculations of the surface atomic concentration from XPS spectra are shown in Table S1. It was found that the surface sulfur concentrations of S0.05-CDGO, S0.10-CDGO, S0.20-CDGO and S0.40-CDGO were 0.6 %, 1.8 %, 3.2 %, and 3.5 %. Fig. 8 shows the TEM and HRTEM images of Sx-CDGO nanocomposites, in which CDs are highlighted in yellow circles. There are complete lamellar structures in S0.05-CDGO (Fig. 8a), S0.10-CDGO (Fig. 8b) and S0.20-CDGO (Fig. 8c), while the morphology of S0.40-CDGO (Fig. 8d) is very fragmented and irregular. From S0.05-CDGO (Fig. 8e) to S0.10-CDGO (Fig. 8f) and S0.20-CDGO (Fig. 8g), the size of the CDs keeps increasing as the electrolyte concentration increases, which could be explained as following. When subjected to constant voltage, the concentration of free radicals in the solution increases if the concentration of the electrolyte increases, and the EC stripping becomes more severe, resulting in a larger particle size of CDs. By extension, the absence of CDs in S0.40-CDGO may because of too drastic EC process, which results in graphene fragments much larger than 10 nm being stripped from the graphite plate instead of CDs, as shown in Fig. 8h.

The open aperture Z-scan tests of Sx-CDGO in DMF at a concentration of 0.15 g/L is shown in Fig. 9 a, and the data are summarized in Table 2 . S0.10-CDGO exhibits the best β eff value at 65 × 10-10 m W−1, while S0.40-CDGO exhibits the worst at 6.5 × 10-10 m W−1. Fig. 9b shows the energy gap of Sx-CDGO calculated from the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S4), the energy gap of S0.05-CDGO, S0.10-CDGO, S0.20-CDGO and S0.40-CDGO are 4.6, 2.6, 2.8 and 3.2 eV, respectively. Fig. 9c shows the relationship between the β eff value, the energy gap and the surface atomic concentration for each Sx-CDGO. When the doping concentration increases from 0.6 % to 1.8 %, the β eff value of Sx-CDGO increases from 15 × 10-10 to 65 × 10-10 m W−1, which is related to the reduction of the energy gap from 4.6 eV to 2.6 eV. The reduction of the energy gap may arise for two reasons. Firstly, under unchanged morphology, the increase in the doping concentration of S atoms leads to more defects and increased carbon vacancies, thereby increasing the valence charge potential and enhancing electron delocalization. [34] Secondly, sulfur has more available valence electrons than carbon and can replenish some of the electron deficiency in CDGO. At a certain concentration, with increasing S doping amount, the Fermi level of Sx-CDGO further upshifts, and the band gap decreases. [35] However, when the sulfur atom doping concentration further increases, the energy gap of Sx-CDGO increases, and the NLO properties decrease instead. The abnormal phenomenon might be caused by the fewer CDs on the surfaces of S0.20-CDGO and S0.40-CDGO (Fig. 8g, h), which makes them function as single materials instead of composites, thus losing the synergistic effects of composites [9]. At the same time, due to its highest electrolyte concentration which causes violent exfoliation of graphite, S0.40-CDGO shows the highest number of graphene layers, as indicated by the lowest I2D/IG ratio in Fig. 9b [6]. As a result, S0.10-CDGO achieves the best NLO properties resulting from the lowest energy gaps with the large β eff (65 × 10-10 m W−1) and Im χ(3) (213 × 10-12 esu) values.
Some results in which the same technique was used are summarized in Table 3 , and our data demonstrate that S-CDGO composites are among the promising materials for this purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OL properties of S-CDGO/PMMA, P-CDGO/PMMA and N-CDGO/PMMA composites
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, regarding the actual application demand, S-CDGO/PMMA composites were fabricated, which exhibits a high transmittance of nearly 70 % at 532 nm (Fig. S5). The OL properties of the S-CDGO/PMMA was investigated, as shown in Fig. 10. In low input fluence, S-CDGO/PMMA exhibits linear absorption, whereas in high input fluence, they exhibit nonlinear absorption, with initial threshold values of 0.05 J cm−2 (Fig. 10 a). With increasing input fluence, transmittance decreases (Fig. 10b), and a minimum transmittance of 21 % and a dynamic range of 0.67 were obtained for S-CDGO/PMMA composites, while the limiting threshold is 0.40 J cm−2. Compared with S-CDGO/PMMA, P-CDGO/PMMA and N-CDGO/PMMA composites were also fabricated, showing similar transmittance but lower NLO performance, with minimum transmittances of 40 % and 35 % and dynamic ranges of 0.40 and 0.46, respectively. Moreover, all the HD-CDGO/PMMA composites show no noticeable damage under laser irradiation at 16 J cm−2, while the laser damage threshold of PMMA film without HD-CDGO is lower than 8 J cm−2, as a result of HD-CDGO's good stability.

A series of HD-CDGO (S-CDGO, P-CDGO and N-CDGO) were synthesized by a facile EC method. The S-CDGO exhibited the best NLO properties among them. Sx-CDGO nanocomposites with different sulfur doping concentrations were prepared to explore the impact of doping concentration on NLO properties. S0.10-CDGO achieved the most considerable β eff value at 65 × 10-10 m W−1 with the lowest energy gap of 2.6 eV. Furthermore, to realize practicality, solutions castings were used to fabricate the HD-CDGO/PMMA composites, and the S-CDGO/PMMA composite exhibited the best OL properties with a minimum transmittance of 21 %, limiting threshold of 0.40 J cm−2, and dynamic range of 0.67, demonstrating its great potential for practical application. This study presents an approach for designing high-performance NLO materials.

Bingxin Mao: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Chen Liang: Funding acquisition. Jian Li: Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Wenyue Dong: Writing – review & editing. Xu Cui: Writing – review & editing. Qian Duan: Data curation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Jilin Science & Technology Department (20190303069SF) for the financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2023.109469.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
